1f-cc4rDK6cVp_nXouloKB_KXpWMbKuWY: Natan Dvir is an educator, artist, and editorial photographer whose work examines the human dimensions of cultural, social, and political issues. Since 2023, he has served as Managing Director of Education at the Houston Center for Photography, following his role establishing the Part-Time Programs at the International Center of Photography in New York. He holds an MBA from Tel Aviv University and an MFA in Photography from the School of Visual Arts. Israel's first Sony Imaging Ambassador, Dvir is represented by Polaris Images and Anastasia Photo. His work has been exhibited internationally in museums, galleries, and festivals across the United States, Europe, South America, Asia, Australia, and Israel, including the Museum of Fine Arts, Houston, and the Triennial of Photography Hamburg. His photographs have appeared in National Geographic, The New York Times, New York Magazine, Der Spiegel, The Guardian, and Le Monde. He has received numerous international honors, including awards from Picture of the Year (POYi), PDN Photo Annual, American Photography, FotoFest Discoveries, and Critical Mass Top 50
1K7dlWJT9zEfiEnfPKoXZfL6zUtqjRr6I: Camilo Gonzalez is a Colombian-born and Houston-based artist, organizer, and educator. Having studied photography and digital media at the University of Houston, his moving image works incorporate hand-drawn animation, assemblage, collage and are often site-specific and presented in public, accessible spaces. His work has been exhibited both individually and as a part of the collaborative Paradox in galleries, museums and film festivals throughout Texas and beyond including Lawndale Art Center, Rice University, Ann Arbor Film Festival, and CineMarfa. In his role as Education and Technical Director at Aurora Picture Show, he supports the visions of exhibiting artists and leads youth programs encouraging curiosity and experimentation in a variety of settings year-round
1iGD5L54nXf2SGuz4gIwTsqw4ePAVfu54: As a painter, my work is deeply influenced by my background as a writer and librarian. In those roles, I developed a deep appreciation for language—the way it shapes meaning and communicates complex ideas. I learned how carefully chosen words can inform, persuade, and evoke emotions, and I came to understand language as both symbolic and communicative. In my painting, I strive to employ a similar sensibility, but with a different vocabulary: one of color, line, and form. Just as a well-crafted poem can reveal something previously unseen, my abstract works aim to create visual narratives that are both familiar and new, offering a space for exploration and interpretation. I use line, color, repetition, and symbolism to construct a visual language that evokes a similar emotional resonance. My process is deeply rooted in revision, layering elements upon elements, often hiding them from view in the final piece—yet they remain discoverable to those who look closely. It is through this iterative layering that the final composition emerges, and only then do I know my role in the creation is finished. From that point, the painting transcends my intentions, becoming part of the viewer’s experience. Their personal histories, symbols, and beliefs infuse the work with new meanings, allowing each observer to interpret the layers in their own unique way. This dynamic exchange between the artwork and its audience is what makes the process of creation so exhilarating for me. In this way, my abstract expressionism becomes a shared journey, inviting others to engage with their own imagination and to see something of themselves in my art. John Bishop is an abstract expressionist painter who creates gestural mixed media work in acrylics, ink, collage, pencil and marker. His work centers on layering color and mark making into work that allows a different experience up close than at a distance. The art draws one deeper into discovery and recognition. Though his passion for art began as a teenager, he was steered away from an artistic career by well-meaning parents. Instead, he became an English teacher and later a librarian, working in five different countries over the course of three decades. Despite this detour, in he made the life-changing decision to dedicate himself to art full time. Drawing on his experience as a creative writer, Bishop applies similar strategies in his painting—crafting layers of meaning and emotion through form, color, and texture. His work has been exhibited locally in Houston, across the U.S., and internationally. In addition to his artistic practice, Bishop is an active member of the art community and founded Aripa Arte, a nonprofit organization that supports Romanian artists. You can explore his work online at, or connect with him on Instagram and Facebook @johnbishopfineart. His studio is located in Houston, where he continues to create and engage with the local and international art scene
1hjy_WcLqmJWRmdOLJTIlfOhWpS4VhXii: The objects I make are rooted in memory, history, form, function, and movement as it pertains to the human experience and the energies left lingering in the materials. The work translates into a language of landscapes, labor and love; it speaks of bodies rhythmically moving and laboring through time animated as the tools we use to help us work more efficiently and with purpose. My work is deeply interwoven with the historical significance of cotton, a fiber that holds great personal and familial meaning for me. Cotton represents the global and regional history of Tejano and Latino families, connecting them through generations of labor harvesting the plant in South Texas fields. Personally, cotton is present in generations of my family and part of my ancestral line. I come from a long line of cotton pickers, and their resilience is present in the work along with the quiet stillness and abundant sweetness of the South Texas monte
1xnziOqHMc7EFiyWnvwqomwGL9MCSSJAW: In life, there are pivotal moments when the present becomes a mirror, reflecting the paths we’ve traveled and prompting us to consider how our experiences shape our futures. This realization ignited in me a desire to surrender control and embrace the unpredictability of material exploration and viewer engagement through my sculptures and installations. My work is deeply rooted in the land—drawing from my origins, my journeys, and my current environment—and emphasizes the interconnectedness of all beings. I engage in relational sculpture, blending materials such as concrete, coffee sacks, and jute/fique twine with metaphorical narratives inspired by the natural world and ancestral cultures. These materials invite tactile interaction, challenging conventional boundaries and encouraging viewers to engage with the work through touch and physical presence. I aspire to create 'open artworks'—pieces that transcend mere visual appreciation, inviting sensory and emotional involvement that allows the viewer to complete the experience. My aim is to dissolve perceived boundaries, fostering a deeper understanding of our identities as individuals, our connections within communities, and our relationship to the natural world and humanity as a whole
1Q_kKx7y1vZlAAWqLsI_M8qrJPG5RXCsE: Bogdan Mihai is a US–Romanian visual artist based in Houston, Texas. His practice spans photography, abstract mixed media, and painting, with a focus on the interplay between form, imagination, and cultural narratives. Working from his studio at Silver Street Studios, Bogdan develops bodies of work that often bring together fragments of urban life, personal memory, and global perspectives, inviting viewers to explore layered visual stories open to multiple interpretations. Since transitioning from a career in television and international media, Bogdan has exhibited his work in the United States, Romania, Argentina, Hungary, and Mexico. His artistic path reflects both his international experiences and his commitment to building connections within the communities where he works and shows his art. In addition to his studio practice, Bogdan is the co-founder of Aripa Arte, an initiative dedicated to supporting Romanian artists in both their creative growth and professional development. Through his work as an artist, curator, and advocate, he seeks to foster meaningful dialogue between artists and audiences, extending the reach of contemporary art across borders
1zZriUZSP2BgbNPpVFgdLDff6isquqMKG: I am a figurative artist that has been living in the Galveston area for the past 20 years. I moved here in 2005 after living in Ireland, England, Taiwan and Korea. Much of my childhood was spent in the United States, and I was born here. However, my Mother is American and my Father is Irish. They had difficulty planting their roots in one place, so we moved every 3-4 years. I competed my BFA in Painting at Dublin, Ireland’s National College of Art and Design and lived there several more years occupying studios at Temple Bar Gallery and Studios and Phoenix Studios. My early work responded to my youth growing up in Asia. I, then, transitioned almost completely into portraiture and figurative painting. My BFA program really discouraged painting, so much of my undergrad work was drawing. After finishing my BFA, I realized how much I loved to paint and decided to experiment and learn as much as I could about painting by myself. I’ve explored numerous styles of paint application from pointillism, expressionism, cubist type work, works on paper, life painting, oils, acrylic, and more. I try to continually learn and improve, so my application of paint seems to evolve with that desire. In 2014, I moved to New York City for three years and obtained my MFA in Painting from the New York Academy of Art. I loved the programs. They offered extensive classical training in anatomy, life drawing, painting, art history, contemporary art studies, composition, portraiture and more. It was really the place for me I felt. I received a lot of training that I wasn’t able to obtain on my own. I was even awarded a residency to Russia. I left New York, in 2017, and moved back to the Galveston area. I have since lectured and exhibited throughout Houston, Galveston and surrounding areas
1Dy879YohshTgIuNMIo9v5ul3ek9-DSQ7:
Mi trabajo es una mezcla de mi pasión por el comportamiento humano y la necesidad de expresar a gritos mis sentimientos.
Una obra llena de color donde materializó lo que  es para mi vivir contento siendo compasivo, amando, sirviendo y agradeciendo profundamente al que nos permite estar.
El darle forma, textura y nombre a mis sentimientos ha sido mi mejor terapia.
1DIgfwyOiniXSCEOcRgAGyNZGxpNp30ox:
Artist: Miguel Ángel Ávila Artists for Venezuela 2026

About Miguel Ángel Ávila is a Mexican artist based in Texas. He’s been a resident of Southwest Houston for almost two decades. Working mostly with acrylics and graphite, his figurative drawings and paintings are inspired by his cultural background, dreamscapes, memories, color, his formal training (architecture), and his experience of growing up in a modern and vibrant metropolis in northern Mexico. Avila’s artwork has been exhibited and collected in North America and Europe
1atcqJVXjpO8CsE_bVjfMdb2Tc65rFEMZ: I am a practicing artist, musician, teacher and story teller that has been creating art since childhood. I spent many of my early years, before obtaining my degrees, raising a family, creating portraits, and teaching art in my children’s schools. At the moment, I am an activities director and art therapist at a local Dickinson home for the elderly. I spent my childhood growing up in the Midwestern United States. I met my Irish husband in my twenties and had the opportunity to live all over the world. I have lived on 5 continents and numerous countries (Ireland, England, Venezuela, Taiwan, Korea, South Africa and the US) and states. I bring my love for art to create and teach classes wherever the road takes me. In 2002, in my fifties, I completed my BFA at Dublin, Ireland’s National College of Art and Design. I was, also, awarded a residency, during that time at The Lyme Academy. I went on to complete my MFA, in 2004, in London, at University of East London. Upon moving to Texas, in 2005, I have taught at public schools as well as local colleges including: Brazosport College and Lee College. I, also, taught at The Ronald McDonald House through the Galveston Art Center
1xUJeztt1Dq9ozlXXTDVqPC9Z3UCL7AKY: ‏Diana-Sofia Estrada is a visual artist/educator born and raised in Houston, Texas. Estrada’s work encompasses paintings, installations, social practice, video, and animation to question our immediate visual culture and environment. Drawing upon her family's Guatemalan heritage, her current work investigates broken familial narratives and visual/atmospheric conditions. She received her BFA in Painting/Drawing and BA in Psychology from U.N.T. and her MFA in Art from the California Institute of the Arts. Estrada has exhibited and screened her work nationally and internationally including Alice Yard Space (Trinidad), L.A.C.E. Exhibitions (Los Angeles, CA), at Artlab at the Smithsonian Hirschhorn Museum ( Washington D.C.), Matucana 100 (Santiago, Chile), Children's Museum of the Arts( New York, NY), Torrance Art Museum (Torrance, CA), and at Wonzimer Gallery (Los Angeles, CA), and with Marta Lidia Umaña-Matta de Wer at San Jacinto College Central Gallery, Pasadena, TX, 2026. Estrada is a member of the artist-run gallery Throughline
1wOM5EiETlkdm2fun-i31muZ79kC07tNs: Isela Aguirre is a multi-disciplinary artist based in Houston, Texas. She uses photography, camera-less photography processes, collage, fiber, painting, and installation in her work. Her work is bold, colorful, and playful with an air of the ephemeral and non-precious. Isela is inspired by the idea that we as individuals are in a constant state of change whether we decide to embrace it or not. She uses nature and her immediate surroundings as her muse. The images she captures are transformed into digital collages, fiber constructions and site specific installations. Isela Aguirre is a native Houstonian. She received her BFA in Studio Photography and her MFA in Painting from the University of Houston. She has received grants to create public installations and has shown her work in solo and group shows in Houston and Austin
1qwvI-pgRoFN3IJhKMhzAFVnlz1RctqzZ: Ellen H Ray Ellen H Ray is an interdisciplinary artist whose practice is rooted in meditation and the study of physical and spiritual connections across life forms. Her work reflects her belief that the Earth, and the universe encompassing it, is a master plan of intelligent design that holds lessons for us about resource sharing, communication, and systems that work in community. She makes drawings, paintings, objects, and installations in which organic and spiritual imagery mix with vibration patterns and other visuals she experiences in her meditation. She is a member of Throughline Collective here in Houston, has shown with many galleries and learning institutions including Monterroso Gallery, Ardest Gallery, Bosque Gallery, Marie Flickenger Fine Arts Center, Dallas Bath House Cultural Center, Throughline Collective, Mönchskirche Cultural Center, Salzwedel Germany. Her work is in public and private collections throughout Texas and beyond
